# React & Web Performance Optimization Techniques

## Frontend System Design + Complete Senior Interview Guide

Web performance is one of the most important **Senior Frontend / React interview topics**.

Interviewers ask this to test:

✅ Deep understanding of browser rendering

✅ Ability to build fast, scalable apps

✅ Knowledge of loading strategies

✅ Advanced JS bundler concepts (webpack, Vite)

✅ Awareness of Core Web Vitals

✅ Real production optimization skills

Below is a **complete detailed article** on the topics you provided, with:

- Explanation
- Real-world examples
- Trade-offs
- Best practices
- Code snippets

Everything you need to answer any interviewer at any level.

Used by:

```txt
Amazon
Netflix
Slack
Facebook
Vercel
LinkedIn
Uber
Airbnb
YouTube
```

---

# 1. Load Script Efficiently With async and defer

## Purpose

Improve page load performance by controlling script loading order.

By default:

```txt
a.js
```

Blocks HTML parsing.

`async` and `defer` change this behavior.

---

## async

- Downloads script in parallel
- Executes as soon as it downloads
- May interrupt DOM parsing

Use when script does not depend on DOM/order.

Great for:

- Analytics
- Ads
- Independent third-party scripts

---

## defer

- Downloads script in parallel
- Executes AFTER DOM is ready
- Preserves order

app.js![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Great for:

- Application scripts
- Scripts requiring DOM
- Preserving execution order

---

## Async vs Defer

| Feature             | async           | defer             |
| ------------------- | --------------- | ----------------- |
| Parallel download   | ✅              | ✅                |
| Blocks HTML parsing | ⚠️ minimal      | ❌                |
| Preserves order     | ❌              | ✅                |
| Executes when       | Immediately     | After HTML parsed |
| Best for            | Ads / analytics | App scripts       |

---

# 2. Tree Shaking

## Purpose

Removes unused code from bundle.

Bundlers like Webpack, Rollup, and Vite analyze imports and eliminate unused modules.

Example:

```js
// utils.js
export const a = () => {};
export const b = () => {};
```

Import only `a`:

```js
import { a } from "./utils";
```

`b` gets removed during build.

---

## Requirements for Tree Shaking

✅ Use ES modules (`import/export`)

❌ CommonJS (`require`) is not shakable

✅ Avoid side effects

✅ Use `sideEffects: false` in package.json

---

## Real-World Impact

- Massive bundle reduction
- Faster page loads
- Reduced memory usage
- Better core web vitals

Used in:

- React
- Lodash-es
- MUI
- Zustand

---

# 3. Code Splitting

## Purpose

Break large bundles into smaller chunks.

Loaded on demand.

Improves:

✅ Initial load time

✅ Time to Interactive

✅ FCP + LCP

---

## Example

Using React lazy:

```jsx
const Profile = React.lazy(() => import("./Profile"));

function App() {
  return (
    <Suspense fallback={<p>Loading...</p>}>
      <Profile />
    </Suspense>
  );
}
```

Bundler splits chunk automatically.

---

## Types

- **Route-based** (Next.js pages)
- **Component-based** (React.lazy)
- **Vendor-based** (`vendor.js`)
- **Modular splitting** for micro-frontends

---

## Real-World Impact

- Amazon reduced initial JS by 60%
- Netflix uses route-based splitting
- LinkedIn uses component-level splitting

---

# 4. Reactivity in JavaScript Frameworks

Reactivity means:

```txt
State changes → UI updates automatically
```

Each framework does this differently.

---

## React

- Virtual DOM
- Reconciliation
- Re-renders components on state change

```jsx
setState(...);
```

Uses:

- Diffing
- Reconciler
- Fiber

---

## Vue

- Uses Proxies to detect state changes
- Fine-grained reactivity

---

## Solid

- Direct signal-based reactivity
- No virtual DOM
- Extremely fast

---

## Svelte

- Compile-time reactivity
- No virtual DOM
- Uses compiler-level updates

---

## Core Idea

Framework reactivity affects:

✅ Performance

✅ Bundle size

✅ Runtime cost

✅ Rerender cost

Modern reactivity trend: **fine-grained + compile-time**.

---

# 5. Pagination — Offset vs Cursor-Based

## Offset Pagination

```txt
GET /items?page=2&size=20
```

Uses `OFFSET` in database.

### Cons

- Slow for large tables
- Data shifts when new items added
- Bad UX with sorting

---

## Cursor-Based Pagination

```txt
GET /items?cursor=xyz&size=20
```

Uses a **cursor** (id/timestamp).

### Pros

✅ Fast

✅ Consistent

✅ Works well with infinite scrolling

✅ Used by Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn

---

## When to Use

- **Offset**: Small tables (blogs, admin dashboards)
- **Cursor**: Large tables (feeds, comments, notifications)

---

# 6. Adaptive Loading

## Purpose

Load different resources based on:

✅ Device capabilities

✅ Network speed

✅ Battery level

✅ CPU

✅ RAM

---

## Example

```jsx
const { effectiveType } = navigator.connection;

if (effectiveType === "4g") {
  loadHDContent();
} else {
  loadLightContent();
}
```

Adaptive loading is used by:

- YouTube
- Netflix
- Amazon
- Facebook

---

# 7. List Virtualization

## Purpose

Only render visible list items.

Reduces:

- DOM nodes
- Reflows
- Memory

---

## Example — react-window

```jsx
import { FixedSizeList } from "react-window";

<FixedSizeList height={400} itemSize={40} itemCount={10000}>
  {({ index, style }) => <div style={style}>Row {index}</div>}
</FixedSizeList>;
```

---

## Used In

- Table libraries
- Chat apps
- Datagrids
- Financial dashboards
- Excel-like apps

---

# 8. Core Web Vitals

Google’s metrics for user experience.

---

## Metrics

- **LCP** (Largest Contentful Paint)
- **INP** (Interaction to Next Paint)
- **CLS** (Cumulative Layout Shift)
- **FCP** (First Contentful Paint)

---

## LCP

Main content should paint in < 2.5s.

## INP

Interaction should respond within 200ms.

## CLS

Page shouldn’t shift unexpectedly.

## FCP

First content should paint quickly.

---

## Improve Core Web Vitals

✅ Use SSR / ISR

✅ Optimize images

✅ Use CDN

✅ Reduce JS

✅ Use lazy loading

✅ Reduce third-party scripts

---

# 9. Static Import

## Purpose

Imports evaluated at compile time:

```js
import { Button } from "./Button";
```

Bundled into main.

---

## Pros

✅ Reliable

✅ Available immediately

## Cons

❌ Increases bundle size

Use only for **critical code**.

---

# 10. Dynamic Import

## Purpose

Load a module on demand.

```js
const module = await import("./chart.js");
```

Or with React:

```jsx
const Chart = React.lazy(() => import("./Chart"));
```

Enables:

✅ Code splitting

✅ Lazy features

✅ Route-based splitting

---

# 11. Optimizing Loading Sequence

## Rule

Load resources in this priority:

1. HTML
2. CSS
3. Above-the-fold images
4. Critical JS
5. Non-critical JS
6. Analytics
7. Ads

Use:

✅ Preload

✅ Defer

✅ Prefetch

✅ Async

---

# 12. Import on Visibility

Load code when a component is visible.

Using IntersectionObserver:

```jsx
const observer = new IntersectionObserver(([entry]) => {
  if (entry.isIntersecting) {
    import("./VideoPlayer.js");
  }
});

observer.observe(ref.current);
```

Used for:

✅ Videos

✅ Charts

✅ Maps

✅ Rare widgets

---

# 13. Import on Interaction

Load feature when user interacts.

Example — Load Emoji Picker only when user opens it:

```jsx
async function openPicker() {
  const module = await import("./EmojiPicker.js");

  module.open();
}
```

Used in:

- Slack
- Twitter
- LinkedIn
- Notion

Great for reducing initial bundle.

---

# 14. Preload and Prefetch

## Preload

Load resources needed for current page:

hero.jpg![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Used for:

✅ Fonts

✅ Hero image

✅ Critical JS

---

## Prefetch

Load resources for future pages:

&lt;link&nbsp; rel="prefetch"&nbsp; href="next.js"/&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Used for:

✅ Next route

✅ Predictive UX

Frameworks like Next.js automatically prefetch route bundles.

---

# 15. Third-Party Libraries Optimization

## Techniques

✅ Lazy load third-party scripts

✅ Use small alternatives (Preact, day.js, zustand)

✅ Load only when needed

✅ Self-host large scripts

✅ Compress and minify

✅ Use CDNs

---

## Real-World Impact

Netflix reduced third-party scripts by:

- Loading YouTube embeds lazily
- Removing Google Tag Manager overhead

Slack reduced payload by:

- Replacing lodash with lodash-es
- Removing unused polyfills

---

# 16. Compression for Better Performance

## Techniques

### 1. Gzip

Older compression, good baseline.

### 2. Brotli (Recommended)

40% smaller than Gzip.

Used by:

- Cloudflare
- Amazon
- Vercel
- CDN networks

Enable in NGINX:

```nginx
brotli on;
brotli_types text/javascript application/json;
```

Reduces bundles significantly.

---

# 17. Efficient Loading of Media

## Techniques

✅ Use WebP / AVIF

✅ Lazy-load images

✅ Use responsive images (`srcset`)

✅ Async video streaming

✅ Optimize hero images

✅ Use HTTP/2

---

## Example

&lt;img&nbsp; src"{"lazy}&nbsp; srcset="&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; image-400.webpw,&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; image-1200.webp 1200w![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Great for:

✅ Better LCP

✅ Faster page loads

✅ Better mobile UX

---

# 18. Periodically Synchronize Data in Background

## Purpose

Keep app data fresh without blocking UI.

Techniques:

✅ Background sync

✅ Service Workers

✅ Web Push

✅ Cache-first + stale-while-revalidate

---

## Example — React Query Background Sync

```jsx
useQuery(["messages"], fetchMessages, {
  refetchInterval: 30000,
});
```

Refreshes every 30 seconds silently.

Used in:

- Slack
- LinkedIn
- Notion
- Instagram
- Trading dashboards

---

# 19. Big Picture Optimization Strategy

Modern frontend performance strategy:

### Rendering

- SSR / ISR / RSC
- Streaming SSR
- Selective hydration

### Loading

- Code splitting
- Dynamic imports
- Prefetch
- Preload

### JS Optimization

- Tree shaking
- Compression
- Remove unused code

### Runtime Optimization

- Virtualization
- Memoization
- Web Workers

### Network

- HTTP/2
- CDN
- Cache-Control headers

### Media

- WebP
- AVIF
- Lazy load

### Monitoring

- Web Vitals
- Real-user monitoring
- Performance profiling

---

# 20. Real-World Enterprise Architecture

For a Netflix-like app:

```txt
SSR + streaming
    +
Code splitting
    +
Prefetch
    +
Dynamic import
    +
Adaptive loading
    +
Media optimization
    +
Cache + CDN
    +
Web Vitals monitoring
    +
Background sync
    +
Third-party optimization
```

Real performance is a **layered strategy**, not a single trick.

---

# 21. Complexity & Impact

Optimizations affect:

- LCP
- INP
- CLS
- FID
- Bundle size
- Server load
- SEO
- Mobile performance
- Conversion rates

**Amazon lost 1% revenue for every extra 100ms of delay.**

---

# 22. Senior React Interview Answer

> Modern React and web performance strategy is a layered architecture — combining code splitting, tree shaking, dynamic imports, preload/prefetch, list virtualization, adaptive loading, Core Web Vitals optimization, media optimization, compression, and background synchronization. Enterprise apps like Amazon, LinkedIn, Netflix, and Vercel apply these together to reduce JS payload, decrease TTI/INP, and increase engagement. Techniques like import on visibility, import on interaction, cursor-based pagination, third-party optimization, and streaming SSR represent the modern industry standard. The right strategy depends on device, network, business goals, and user experience metrics — but the underlying principle remains the same: deliver the least amount of code, at the right time, from the closest location, with predictable rendering to give the fastest experience possible.

# Advanced Frontend Performance Optimization Deep Dive

## Code Splitting with React.lazy • Tree Shaking • Adaptive Loading

These are the **most important Senior React / Frontend interview topics** because they directly impact:

✅ Bundle size

✅ Time to Interactive (TTI)

✅ Web Vitals (LCP, FCP, INP)

✅ Mobile performance

✅ Enterprise scale

✅ SEO ranking

✅ Business KPIs

Interviewers ask this to test:

✅ Understanding of module bundling

✅ ES module semantics

✅ Real-world performance experience

✅ Deep browser + network knowledge

✅ Ability to design fast, scalable apps

Used in production at:

```txt
Amazon
Netflix
LinkedIn
Vercel
Slack
Uber
Airbnb
Twitter
```

Let’s break each part clearly and deeply.

---

# 1. Code Splitting with React.lazy — Complete Deep Dive

## What is Code Splitting?

Code splitting means:

```txt
Breaking one large JS bundle into smaller chunks
      ↓
Loading each chunk on demand
```

This solves the problem where React apps often ship massive bundles (2MB+) that slow down initial load.

Result:

- Faster first paint
- Faster Time to Interactive
- Improved Web Vitals
- Better mobile UX
- Smaller memory usage

Frameworks that support code splitting:

- Webpack
- Vite
- Rollup
- Next.js
- Remix
- CRA
- Parcel

---

## Types of Code Splitting

### 1. Route-based Code Splitting

Load only the chunk for the current route.

Great for:

- Multi-page React apps
- Dashboards

### 2. Component-based Code Splitting

Load individual components dynamically.

Great for:

- Modals
- Charts
- Editors
- Rich components

### 3. Vendor Code Splitting

Split React, Lodash, chart libraries etc.

Great for:

- Optimizing caching
- Long-term performance

---

## How React.lazy Works

`React.lazy()` allows importing a component asynchronously.

Bundler splits it into a separate JS chunk.

React downloads and mounts it only when needed.

Uses:

- Dynamic import
- Promises
- React Suspense
- Fallback UI

---

## Example — Basic React.lazy

```jsx
import React, { Suspense, lazy } from "react";

const Profile = lazy(() => import("./Profile"));

function App() {
  return (
    <Suspense fallback={<p>Loading...</p>}>
      <Profile />
    </Suspense>
  );
}

export default App;
```

Result:

- `Profile.js` is split into its own chunk.
- Downloaded only when `App` renders.
- Displays fallback while loading.
- Great for slow networks.

---

## Example — Route-Based Splitting

```jsx
import React, { Suspense, lazy } from "react";

import { BrowserRouter, Routes, Route } from "react-router-dom";

const Home = lazy(() => import("./pages/Home"));

const Profile = lazy(() => import("./pages/Profile"));

const Settings = lazy(() => import("./pages/Settings"));

function App() {
  return (
    <BrowserRouter>
      <Suspense fallback={<p>Loading...</p>}>
        <Routes>
          <Route path="/" element={<Home />} />
          <Route path="/profile" element={<Profile />} />
          <Route path="/settings" element={<Settings />} />
        </Routes>
      </Suspense>
    </BrowserRouter>
  );
}
```

Each route creates:

- home.chunk.js
- profile.chunk.js
- settings.chunk.js

Result:

- Amazing performance
- Load only what user needs
- Standard architecture in large apps

Used in Vercel dashboard, LinkedIn feed, Slack workspace.

---

## Advanced React.lazy Examples

### Example 1 — Delayed loading (chunk name)

```jsx
const Chart = lazy(
  () =>
    import(
      /* webpackChunkName: "chart" */
      "./Chart"
    ),
);
```

Improves debugging.

Bundlers name chunks for better dev experience.

---

### Example 2 — Multiple lazy components

```jsx
const Modal = lazy(() => import("./Modal"));

const Notifications = lazy(() => import("./Notifications"));

<Suspense fallback={<Skeleton />}>
  <Modal />
  <Notifications />
</Suspense>;
```

React Suspense wraps them together.

---

### Example 3 — Preload before user needs

```jsx
const Chart = lazy(() => import("./Chart"));

function preload() {
  import("./Chart");
}
```

Trigger `preload()` on hover:

```jsx
<button onMouseEnter={preload}>Show Chart</button>
```

Speeds up perceived performance.

---

## Suspense Fallback Considerations

Fallback should be:

✅ Small

✅ Consistent

✅ Skeleton

✅ Not intrusive

Bad fallback:

```jsx
<p>Loading everything...</p>
```

Great fallback:

```jsx
<Skeleton width={200} height={80} />
```

Reduces CLS.

---

## Code Splitting Benefits

✅ Faster initial load

✅ Small initial JS

✅ Reduced TTI

✅ Better mobile UX

✅ Better core web vitals

✅ Higher user engagement

✅ Higher conversion

---

## Common Mistakes

❌ Splitting too small components (chunk overhead)

❌ No Suspense fallback

❌ Splitting critical UI

❌ Not preloading commonly used routes

❌ Ignoring cache boundaries

---

## Real-World Examples

- **Amazon**: Uses code splitting on product pages
- **Netflix**: Route-based splitting
- **LinkedIn**: Component-level splitting
- **Slack**: Rare emoji picker loaded only on click
- **Vercel Dashboard**: Fully route-split

Result: sub-second interactions on large enterprise apps.

---

# 2. Tree Shaking — Complete Deep Dive

## What is Tree Shaking?

Tree shaking is a compiler-level optimization that removes:

```txt
Unused exports
Dead code
Modules never imported
```

Bundlers like Webpack, Rollup, Vite, esbuild use it during production build.

Result:

- Smaller bundles
- Faster load
- Better performance

---

## Analogy

Imagine a bookshelf with 100 books.

You only read 10.

Tree shaking is the process of shipping only those 10 books instead of all 100.

---

## Requirements for Tree Shaking

Tree shaking only works if:

✅ Uses ES Modules (`import`/`export`)

✅ Static structure (no dynamic requires)

✅ Package marked `"sideEffects": false`

✅ Uses dead-code elimination-friendly library

❌ CommonJS (`require`) breaks tree shaking

---

## Example — Tree Shaking Works

```js
// utils.js
export function add(a, b) {
  return a + b;
}

export function subtract(a, b) {
  return a - b;
}
```

Import only `add`:

```js
import { add } from "./utils";
```

Bundler removes `subtract` at build.

Result: smaller bundle.

---

## Example — Tree Shaking Breaks

Using CommonJS:

```js
// utils.js
module.exports = {
  add,
  subtract,
};
```

Import only `add`:

```js
const { add } = require("./utils");
```

Tree shaking does NOT work.

Full module included.

Bundle bloats.

---

## sideEffects Flag

Bundlers rely on this in `package.json`:

```json
{
  "sideEffects": false
}
```

Meaning:

- Modules do not modify global state
- Safe to eliminate

Otherwise, bundler cannot safely remove code.

Add allow list:

```json
{
  "sideEffects": ["./polyfills.js", "./analytics.js"]
}
```

Great optimization.

---

## Tree Shakable Libraries

Modern libraries are tree-shakable:

✅ Lodash-es
✅ MUI (v5)
✅ Chakra UI
✅ Ant Design (with babel plugin)
✅ Zustand
✅ Recoil
✅ Radix UI

Import optimized:

```js
import Button from "lodash-es/debounce";
```

Instead of:

```js
import _ from "lodash";
```

Which imports the entire library.

---

## Real-World Impact

Netflix reduced JS by 40% using:

- Tree shaking
- Removing side-effect imports
- Using lodash-es

Airbnb reduced bundle size by:

- Migrating to ES Modules
- Using modular imports

Tree shaking often shaves **200KB–1MB** off production builds.

---

## Benefits of Tree Shaking

✅ Smaller JS payload

✅ Faster load

✅ Lower memory usage

✅ Cleaner build output

✅ Better web vitals

✅ Better SEO

✅ Lower hosting cost

✅ Better mobile UX

---

## Common Mistakes

❌ Using CommonJS

❌ Legacy libraries without ES modules

❌ Not enabling `sideEffects: false`

❌ Importing entire library (`import _ from "lodash"`)

❌ Not using tree-shakable UI kits

❌ Poor bundler configuration

Fix these and bundle size drops significantly.

---

# 3. Adaptive Loading Techniques

## What is Adaptive Loading?

Adaptive loading means:

```txt
Load different resources based on user's device or network conditions
```

Different users → different UX capabilities.

Example:

- 4G user → HD images
- 2G user → optimized text-only

Reduces friction.

Improves Web Vitals dramatically for users with:

- Older devices
- Weak networks
- Low memory
- Small bandwidth

Used by:

- YouTube
- Netflix
- Amazon
- Instagram
- Facebook

Google promotes this heavily.

---

## Adaptive Loading Techniques

Let’s go over each:

---

## 1. Network-Aware Loading

Use Network Information API:

```js
const { effectiveType } = navigator.connection;
```

Values:

- slow-2g
- 2g
- 3g
- 4g

Example:

```js
if (effectiveType === "4g") {
  loadHDVideo();
} else {
  loadLightVideo();
}
```

---

## 2. Save-Data Header

Users can enable "Save Data" in browser.

Detect it:

```js
navigator.connection.saveData;
```

Then:

```js
if (navigator.connection.saveData) {
  loadLowResImage();
}
```

Used by Facebook Lite, YouTube Go.

---

## 3. Device Memory Adaptive Loading

Detect RAM:

```js
navigator.deviceMemory;
```

Example:

```js
if (navigator.deviceMemory < 2) {
  disableAnimations();
}
```

Great for older devices.

Used by:

- Facebook Lite
- Twitter Lite
- Instagram Lite

---

## 4. CPU Adaptive Loading

Detect logical CPU cores:

```js
navigator.hardwareConcurrency;
```

Example:

```js
if (navigator.hardwareConcurrency < 4) {
  disableHeavyEffects();
}
```

Used by:

- Airbnb search page
- Netflix video engine

---

## 5. Adaptive Image Loading

Serve smaller images to slow devices:

&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; small.jpg&nbsp; 300w,&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; medium.jpg 800w,&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; large.jpg&nbsp; 1200w![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Also:

- WebP for modern devices
- AVIF for latest browsers

Used by:

- Amazon
- Nike
- Booking.com

---

## 6. Adaptive JS Loading

Send lighter JS to weak devices.

Example:

- 4G → full React app
- 2G → server-rendered light HTML

Used by:

- Vercel
- Facebook Lite

---

## 7. Adaptive Video Streaming

Adaptive bitrate (ABR) streaming:

- HLS
- DASH

Video quality dynamically adjusts.

Used by:

- YouTube
- Netflix
- Prime Video
- Zoom
- Google Meet

---

## 8. Adaptive Fonts

Load lighter fonts based on connection:

```js
if (navigator.connection.saveData) {
  loadSystemFont();
} else {
  loadCustomFont();
}
```

Fonts are one of the biggest performance killers.

---

## 9. Adaptive Rendering

Reduce rendering cost:

- Simplify animations
- Reduce parallax
- Remove heavy shadows
- Reduce DOM depth

For low-end devices.

---

## 10. Adaptive Fetch

Reduce data fetched:

```js
if (navigator.connection.saveData) {
  fetch("/lightweight-api");
} else {
  fetch("/full-api");
}
```

Reduces payload size.

---

## Adaptive Loading — Real-World Examples

### Netflix

- Detects device performance
- Switches video quality
- Uses adaptive JS

### YouTube

- Uses AVIF, WebP, JPEG XL
- Adaptive video streaming
- Reduces UI complexity on weak devices

### Facebook

- Different app version for slow networks
- Adaptive JS bundle
- Adaptive image loading

### Amazon

- Adaptive product images
- Adaptive homepage rendering
- Adaptive checkout flow

---

## Benefits of Adaptive Loading

✅ Better mobile UX

✅ Better Web Vitals

✅ Better retention

✅ Better SEO

✅ Better performance in emerging markets

✅ Better inclusivity

---

## Common Mistakes

❌ Ignoring low-end devices

❌ Serving giant images to weak networks

❌ Loading heavy JS on 2G

❌ Not respecting Save Data

❌ Not testing with slow throttled network

Fixing these dramatically improves performance.

---

## Complexity Recap

### Code Splitting

- Time: O(chunks)
- Bundle size: Reduces dramatically
- Load time: Improves

### Tree Shaking

- Time: Build-time analysis
- Bundle size: Reduces
- Runtime: Faster JS execution

### Adaptive Loading

- Time: O(1) checks
- Runtime cost: Small
- Performance: Massive improvement

---

# 4. Enterprise Interview Answer

> Code splitting with React.lazy allows large apps to load only the code needed for the current route or interaction, improving TTI, LCP, and mobile performance. Tree shaking eliminates unused exports during the production build, which requires ES modules and correctly configured side-effect boundaries. Adaptive loading further optimizes the experience by serving different resources based on device capabilities, network speed, memory, and CPU — allowing apps like Netflix, YouTube, and Amazon to deliver rich experiences to fast connections while remaining usable on 2G networks. In enterprise React apps, these techniques are combined to build fast, resilient, and scalable systems that maintain excellent performance across billions of users worldwide.
